claim: In bcc iron, Mn decreases density ρ linearly with increasing concentration, as reported by Zwell et al.
evidence_text: Zwell et al. found that Mn decreases ρ linearly with increasing concentration [5].

claim: In bcc iron, Ti decreases density ρ, as shown by Zwell and Wriedt.
evidence_text: Zwell and Wriedt showed that Ti decreases ρ [6].

claim: In bcc iron, Be and Si decrease the lattice parameter a, while Al, Ti, V, Ge, and Mo increase a, according to Takeuchi et al.
evidence_text: Takeuchi et al. found that Be and Si decrease a whereas Al, Ti, V, Ge, and Mo raise a [3].

claim: In bcc iron, Ni increases the lattice parameter a up to an atomic mass fraction of about 15%, beyond which it reduces a, as found by Zwell et al.
evidence_text: Zwell et al. showed that Ni increases a to an atomic mass fraction of about 15 %, then reduces a [4].

claim: In bcc iron, the dependence of density ρ and lattice parameter a on atomic mass fraction is linear up to 10% for several elements: Co does not affect ρ, Cr decreases ρ, while Re, Ir, Pt, Ru, and Rh increase ρ.
evidence_text: Zwell et al. found a linear dependence of a and ρ of bcc iron on an atomic mass fraction of up to 10 % of several elements: Co has no effect on ρ, Cr decreases ρ, Re, Ir, Pt, Ru, and Rh increase ρ [8].

claim: The density ρ of bcc iron decreases linearly with Si atomic mass fraction up to 5%; for higher Si concentrations, ρ is influenced by the cooling rate after annealing, as reported by Richter et al.
evidence_text: ρ is shown to decrease linearly with an atomic mass fraction of Si up to 5%; for higher concentrations ρ is influenced by the cooling rate after annealing [11].

claim: For several melts of two heat-resistant low alloy steels, the influence of changing composition on physical properties including density dominates over the influence of changes in microstructure, as stated by Richter.
evidence_text: For several melts of two heat-resistant low alloy steels, Richter stated that the influence of changing composition on the physical properties including ρ dominates over changes in microstructure [13].

claim: Regression analysis for individual steel qualities shows that additions of Cr and Si reduce the density ρ.
evidence_text: He studied the influence of composition separately for each steel quality. The regression results show the reduction in ρ by addition of Cr and Si.

claim: The effect of C on the density of martensite (α-iron+Fe3C) has been studied and validated against experimental data.
evidence_text: The effect of C on the density of martensite, respectively, α-iron+Fe3C has been studied in [18] and the result has been checked versus experimental data in [20].

claim: Phosphorus decreases the lattice parameter a of bcc iron linearly with increasing concentration, as found by Gale and confirmed by Hornbogen.
evidence_text: Gale found that P decreases a linearly with increasing concentration [1]. Hornbogen confirmed this result [2].

claim: The effect of C on the density of martensite (α-iron+Fe3C) has been studied and checked versus experimental data.
evidence_text: The effect of C on the density of martensite, respectively, α -iron+Fe3C has been studied in [18] and the result has been checked versus experimental data in [20].

claim: Only data that included the exact composition for each sample were considered because data based on standard compositions were inadequate for accurate density prediction.
evidence_text: Data on properties of steels often are given with respect to the standard compositions. Then, a higher accuracy than two decimals (if ρ is given in g/cm^3) is not adequate. Using these data for the present computations was unsuccessful. So only data that included the exact composition for each sample could be considered.

claim: Richter gave a maximum experimental error for the density of ±0.01%.
evidence_text: Richter gave a maximum experimental error for the density of ±0.01% [13; 14].

claim: The present analysis uses the regression formula 1/ρ_x = Σ r_i C_i^wt(x), where r_i is the regression coefficient of element i and r_i ≅ 1/ρ_i for substitutional solutes.
evidence_text: In the present analysis the following regression formula was used: \frac {1}{\rho_{x}} = \sum_{i} r_{i} C_{i}^{\mathrm{wt}} (x) where r_i is the regression coefficient of element i and r_i \cong 1 / \rho_i for substitutional solutes [23].

claim: The theoretical regression coefficient for carbon r_C is 1.750·10^{-4} m^3/kg, derived from the densities of cementite and iron and the stoichiometry 1 kg C + 13.949 kg Fe → 14.949 kg Fe3C.
evidence_text: r_{\mathrm{C}} = \left(\frac{14.949}{\rho_{\mathrm{Fe}_3\mathrm{C}}} - \frac{13.949}{\rho_{\mathrm{Fe}}}\right) = 1.750 \cdot 10^{-4} \frac{\mathrm{m}^3}{\mathrm{kg}}

claim: For ferritic steels Model II, a thermodynamic analysis was performed where the amount of cementite and solubilities of elements in cementite and ferrite were computed, and the non-linear influence of Cr and Mn on cementite density was included by interpolation of literature data.
evidence_text: For the second model a thermodynamic analysis was performed. The amount of cementite and the solubilities of the elements in cementite and ferrite were computed for the equilibrium...The non-linear influence of Cr and Mn on the density of cementite was computed by interpolation of the data given in [25].

claim: The density or lattice parameter of pure γ-iron at room temperature has to be estimated by extrapolation from alloy series, and Bendick et al. collected literature data including contradicting estimates and studied the anomalous thermal expansion of fcc ferrous alloys in the paramagnetic temperature range.
evidence_text: For that reason the density or lattice parameter of pure γ-iron at room temperature has to be estimated by extrapolation from alloy series. Bendick et al. have collected literature data including contradicting estimates of the lattice parameter of γ-iron and studied the anomalous thermal expansion of fcc ferrous alloys in the paramagnetic temperature range [26], table 4.

claim: Table 2 presents the regression coefficients r_i, apparent densities 1/r_i, deviations from ideal behaviour, and 2σ uncertainties for elements in ferritic and low alloy steels according to Model I with zero solubility in cementite; Cu shows a notable increase in density (apparent 11796 kg/m^3, +32.2% deviation) while Si shows a large decrease (apparent 4200 kg/m^3, +80.3% deviation caused by covalent bonds).
evidence_text: Table 2. Influence of composition on the density r_{stecl} of ferritic and low alloy steels, zero solubility in cementite

claim: Table 3 gives the regression coefficients for the density of ferrite ρ_a when the density of cementite is computed separately (Model II); the apparent density of Cu is 11678 kg/m^3 (+30.9% deviation) and Si is 4300 kg/m^3 (+84.5% deviation).
evidence_text: Table 3. Influence of composition on the density of ferrite ρ_a in ferritic and low alloy steels, density of cementite computed separately

claim: In Model I with zero solubility in cementite and zero C solubility in ferrite, regression coefficients from Table 2 show density and apparent density match within statistical error, except Si (strong deviation) and Mn (deviates by 6σ, outlier criterion >3σ).
evidence_text: The coefficients r_j ≈ 1/ρ_i as explained by equation (4) with x = steel for the assumption of zero solubility in cementite and zero solubility of C in ferrite are given in table 2. Density and apparent density match each other within the statistical error with the exception of Si (strong deviation) and Mn. The coefficient found for Mn deviates with 6σ (usual criteria for an outlier: deviation >3σ).

claim: In Model I, Cu and Mo increase ρ_steel; C, Cr, Mn, Ni, S, Si, and V reduce ρ_steel; Al, Nb, P, and Ti are insignificant; R² = 0.987, maximum deviation 5.9 kg/m³, average deviation 2.0 kg/m³.
evidence_text: Al, Nb, P, and Ti are insignificant in the amounts present in this study. Cu and Mo increase ρ_steel ; C, Cr, Mn, Ni, S, Si, and V reduce ρ_steel . The value of R² equals 0.987. The maximum deviation is 5.9 kg/m³ ; the average deviation is 2.0 kg/m³.

claim: Replacing the regression coefficient for Mo with its theoretical value (1/ρ_Mo) yields a model with R²=0.976, maximum deviation 11.7 kg/m³, average deviation 3.3 kg/m³; neglecting Mo yields R²=0.94, maximum deviation 14.4 kg/m³, average deviation 5.6 kg/m³.
evidence_text: If the regression coefficient for only one single element in substitutional solution is replaced with the theoretical coefficient a useful model is obtained. This is illustrated for Mo, r_Mo = l/r_Mo R² equals 0.976. The model predicts the density of the steels with a maximum deviation of 11.7 kg/m³ and an average deviation of 3.3 kg/m³ ; the result is reasonable. Although the mass content of Mo in the study is maximum 1.1 % and 0.4 % on average, neglecting it gives a model with R² = 0.94 , a maximum deviation of 14.4 kg/m³ and an average deviation of 5.6 kg/m³.

claim: For substitutional solutes with no regression coefficient, theoretical or literature values (1/ρ_i) can and should be used to obtain reasonable density estimates, as replacing r_i with 1/ρ_i for other substitutional solutes yields similar accuracy.
evidence_text: Replacing r_i with l/ρ_i for other substitutional solutes leads to models with similar accuracy. So, for substitutional solutes where no regression coefficient is determined theoretical or literature values can and should be used to get reasonable estimations of the density.

claim: Model II (thermodynamic cementite calculation via Thermo-Calc) yields regression results in Table 3; Cu and Mo increase ρ_ferrite; C, Cr, Mn, Ni, S, Si, and V reduce ρ_ferrite; Al, Nb, P, Ti insignificant; R²=0.994, maximum deviation 4.2 kg/m³, average deviation 1.4 kg/m³.
evidence_text: Model II. The result for the case where the solubilities of Cr and Mn in cementite were computed with Thermo-Calc is given in table 3. ... Al, Nb, P, and Ti are insignificant in the amounts present in this study. Cu and Mo increase ρ_ferrite ; C, Cr, Mn, Ni, S, Si, and V reduce ρ_ferrite . The model has R² = 0.994 , a maximum deviation of 4.2 kg/m³ and an average deviation of 1.4 kg/m³.

claim: Figure 1 plots the inverse of regression coefficients (apparent density) with 95% probability error bars compared to elemental densities and literature values for ferrite in Model II.
evidence_text: Figure 1 shows the inverse of the regression coefficients r_i (apparent density) with error-bars for 95% probability compared to the densities of the elements and literature values (see table 3)

claim: Cementite density is modeled by two linear relations: ρ_cem = 7742 - 687·C_Cr^wt kg/m³ (Eq. 6) for C_Mn^wt in cementite above 0.09 (constant 7680 kg/m³ below), and ρ_cem = 7770 - 741·C_Mn^wt kg/m³ (Eq. 7) for C_Mn^wt above 0.12, with no mixed cases; shown in Figure 2.
evidence_text: The density of cementite in kg/m³ was computed as: ρ_ cementite = (7 7 4 2 - 6 8 7 · C _ Cr ^ wt) (6) for C_Mn^wt in cementite above 0.09, below ρ_cem = 7680 kg/m³ constant, or ρ_ cementite = (7 7 7 0 - 7 4 1 · C _ Mn ^ wt) (7) for C_Mn^wt in cementite above 0.12 (there were no mixed cases), figure 2.

claim: Figure 2 shows the density of cementite with structures Pnma and Pbnm as a function of Cr and Mn content, respectively.
evidence_text: Figure 2. Density of cementite with structures Pnma and Pbnm as a function of Cr- respectively Mn-content.

claim: For 64 batches of austenitic steel, using equation (4) with x=austenite and TiC mass content as variable: TiC, C, Cr, Mn, N, Si, Ti reduce ρ_austenite; Co, Cu, Mo, Ni increase ρ_austenite; R²=0.975, max deviation 10.4 kg/m³, average deviation 2.6 kg/m³; results in Table 4 and Figure 3.
evidence_text: Austenitic steels. 64 batches of steel have been studied by use of equation (4) with x = austenite, including the mass content of TiC as a variable. Solutes in TiC were neglected. The results are shown in table 4 and figure 3. TiC, C, Cr, Mn, N, Si, and Ti reduce ρ_austenite and Co, Cu, Mo, and Ni increase ρ_austenite . The value of R² is 0.975. The maximum deviation is 10.4 kg/m³ , the average deviation is 2.6 kg/m³ .

claim: The significant deviation of the V coefficient in Model II may be due to low V content (average 0.08 mass%), causing slow diffusion and possibly non-equilibrium; the assumption of only ferrite and cementite phases may not be valid for all V-containing steels.
evidence_text: V appeared with an average mass content of 0.08 % in the investigated steels. Low concentration means low concentration gradients thus diffusion processes are slow. The thermodynamic analysis assumes thermodynamic equilibrium but it is not sure that this is achieved for elements present in such small amounts. Additionally, the effect of elements other than Cr and Mn in cementite is neglected due to lack of data. ... Finally, the assumption that the carbide precipitation is at the beginning of the carbide sequence and only the two phases ferrite and cementite are present might not be fulfilled for all V-containing steels.

claim: From regression for austenitic steels, γ-Fe has a regression coefficient r_i = 1.231×10^{-4} m³/kg giving apparent density 8124 kg/m³, with a deviation of +0.05% from the average of literature values (8256, 8132, 8146, 8024, 8070, 8114.5, 8099.79 kg/m³) and 2σ/r_i = 0.6%.
TiC in austenitic steels has a regression coefficient r_i = 2.032×10^{-4} m³/kg and apparent density 4921 kg/m³, close to its elemental density 4910 kg/m³ (deviation 0.2%), but with 2σ/r_i = 17.6%.

Carbon not bound in TiC in austenitic steels has a regression coefficient r_i = 3.178×10^{-4} m³/kg and apparent density 3146 kg/m³, differing by +38.8% from elemental C density 2267 kg/m³, with 2σ/r_i = 42.0%.

Si in austenitic steels has a regression coefficient r_i = 2.436×10^{-4} m³/kg and apparent density 4105 kg/m³, deviating +76.2% from elemental density 2330 kg/m³ due to covalent bonds, with 2σ/r_i = 2.8%, and literature apparent density values of 518 and 1276 kg/m³.

Cr in austenitic steels has regression coefficient r_i = 1.431×10^{-4} m³/kg, apparent density 6986 kg/m³, deviation -2.2% from elemental density 7140 kg/m³, 2σ/r_i = 1.8%, with literature apparent densities 6959 and 7342 kg/m³.
Equation (4) with the regression coefficients from Table 2 (ferritic steels) or Table 4 (austenitic steels) can be used for density estimation of ferritic or austenitic steels.
evidence_text: For an estimation of the density of ferritic or austenitic steels, equation (4) with the regression coefficients r_i in table 2, respectively, table 4 can be used.

claim: For improved accuracy for ferritic steels and for Ti-stabilized austenitic steels, a thermodynamic analysis must be performed; for ferritic steels the density of cementite is computed by equations (6) and (7) and the density of ferrite by equation (4) using the coefficients r_i in Table 3.
evidence_text: For improved accuracy for ferritic steels and for Ti-stabilized austenitic steels, a thermodynamic analysis has to be performed. Then, for ferritic steels the density of cementite has to be computed by equations (6) and (7) and the density of ferrite by equation (4) using the coefficients r_i in table 3.
